1:
"Unto the angel of the church of Ephesus write; These things saith he that holdeth the seven stars in his right hand, who walketh in the midst of the seven golden candlesticks;" The witnesses note the message to Ephesus from him who holds the stars and walks among the lampstands — opening the question of the seven churches' scope. On "unto the angel of the church of Ephesus write," the grammatical voices note the address. Barnes and Robertson note the form: "unto the angel (the minister, v.20) of the church of Ephesus write" — the first of the seven letters is addressed to the angel (minister) of Ephesus; each letter follows the same pattern.

On the scope of the seven churches, interpreters note the frames. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hold the seven churches are literal, historical congregations in Asia, each with its own real condition; the messages, while applicable to all churches, were addressed to actual churches. The latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede (and the later prophetic-historical tradition) take the seven churches as also representing, by the number seven, the whole church, and some read them as successive ages or types of the church through history; the seven are at once literal and representative of the universal church in all times.

On "he that holdeth the seven stars... who walketh in the midst of the seven golden candlesticks," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the self-designation: Christ describes himself (from ch. 1) as the one who "holdeth the seven stars (the ministers) in his right hand" and "walketh in the midst of the seven golden candlesticks (the churches)" — each letter opens with a title of Christ drawn from the vision, suited to the church addressed; here, his holding the ministers and walking among the churches assures his presence and oversight. The verse displays the message to Ephesus from him who holds the stars and walks among the lampstands.

The witnesses agree the substance: the letter to Ephesus opens with Christ's self-designation as holder of the stars and walker among the lampstands, assuring his presence with his churches (Barnes, Robertson, Cyril). On the scope of the seven churches the frames differ: literal historical congregations (Barnes, Robertson) or, with the number seven, also representative of the whole church and (in the prophetic tradition) successive ages (Victorinus, Bede). The verse displays the seven-churches scope. The verse marks the address: the angel of Ephesus. The verse displays the title: holder of the stars. The verse displays the presence: walks among the lampstands. The verse displays the frame: literal and representative. The message displays Christ present among his churches. The verse displays the Ephesus: addressed. The verse displays the stars: held. The verse displays the lampstands: walked among. The message to Ephesus from him who holds the stars and walks among the lampstands displays the opening of the seven letters: Christ, holding the ministers and walking among the churches, addresses the angel of Ephesus — the seven churches read as literal congregations (Barnes, Robertson) and, by the number seven, as representative of the whole church (Victorinus, Bede). The verse displays the present Christ, who knows Ephesus's works (v.2).
2:
"I know thy works, and thy labour, and thy patience, and how thou canst not bear them which are evil: and thou hast tried them which say they are apostles, and are not, and hast found them liars:" The witnesses note Christ's commendation of Ephesus's works, patience, and discernment. On "I know thy works, and thy labour, and thy patience," the grammatical voices note the divine knowledge. Barnes and Robertson note the searching commendation: "I know (with perfect knowledge) thy works, and thy labour (toil), and thy patience (patient endurance)" — Christ knows fully the Ephesian church's deeds, hard work, and steadfast endurance; he commends their diligence and perseverance.

On "how thou canst not bear them which are evil," the witnesses note their intolerance of evil. Barnes and Ellicott note that the church "canst not bear (tolerate) them which are evil" — they would not endure wicked men or false teachers in their midst; their zeal for purity and truth is praised.

On "thou hast tried them which say they are apostles, and are not, and hast found them liars," the grammatical-historical note the discernment: the church "hast tried (tested) them which say they are apostles, and are not, and hast found them liars" — they tested false claimants to apostleship and exposed them as liars; their vigilant discernment, testing the spirits (cf. 1 John 4:1), is commended. The verse displays Christ's commendation of Ephesus's works, patience, and discernment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the knowledge — "I know thy works, and thy labour, and thy patience," Christ commending their deeds, toil, and endurance (Barnes, Robertson); "canst not bear them which are evil," their intolerance of wicked men and false teachers (Barnes, Ellicott); "tried them which say they are apostles... found them liars," their discernment testing and exposing false apostles (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commendation. The verse marks the knowledge: I know thy works. The verse displays the labour: and patience. The verse displays the intolerance: of evil. The verse displays the discernment: tried false apostles. The commendation displays Ephesus diligent, enduring, and discerning. The verse displays the works: known. The verse displays the patience: praised. The verse displays the liars: exposed. Christ's commendation of Ephesus's works, patience, and discernment displays the praise of the diligent church: he knows their works, labour, and endurance, their refusal to tolerate evil, and their testing and exposing of false apostles. The verse displays the commended church, also praised for bearing patiently (v.3).
3:
"And hast borne, and hast patience, and for my name's sake hast laboured, and hast not fainted." The brief verse notes Ephesus's patient endurance for Christ's name. The grammatical voices note the further commendation. Barnes and Robertson note the praise of their perseverance: "hast borne (endured, carried burdens), and hast patience (patient endurance), and for my name's sake hast laboured (toiled), and hast not fainted (grown weary)" — the Ephesian church has endured hardship, persevered patiently, toiled for Christ's name, and not grown weary; their steadfast labour is commended.

On "for my name's sake hast laboured," the witnesses note the motive. Barnes and Ellicott note that their labour was "for my name's sake" — done for Christ, out of love and zeal for his name; not for self but for him; the right motive ennobles their toil.

On "hast not fainted," the grammatical-historical note the perseverance: they "hast not fainted (not grown weary, not given up)" — despite trials and toil, they had not flagged; their endurance had not failed; the commendation of unwearied faithfulness (which makes the rebuke that follows, v.4, the more striking). The verse displays Ephesus's patient endurance for Christ's name.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the perseverance — "hast borne, and hast patience, and for my name's sake hast laboured, and hast not fainted," the church enduring, persevering, toiling for Christ, and not growing weary (Barnes, Robertson); "for my name's sake hast laboured," the labour for Christ, the right motive (Barnes, Ellicott); "hast not fainted," unwearied faithfulness despite trials (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the enduring church. The verse marks the bearing: endured. The verse displays the patience: persevered. The verse displays the motive: for my name. The verse displays the perseverance: not fainted. The commendation displays Ephesus toiling unwearied for Christ. The verse displays the bearing: praised. The verse displays the labour: for Christ. The verse displays the fainting: avoided. Ephesus's patient endurance for Christ's name displays the further commendation of the church: they had borne burdens, persevered, laboured for Christ's name, and not grown weary — unwearied faithfulness. The verse displays the enduring church, against which one thing is held (v.4).
4:
"Nevertheless I have somewhat against thee, because thou hast left thy first love." The witnesses note the rebuke of Ephesus for leaving its first love. On "I have somewhat against thee," the grammatical voices note the turn to reproof. Barnes and Robertson note the qualification of the praise: "nevertheless I have somewhat (this) against thee" — despite all the commendation, Christ has a complaint; the one serious fault that mars the church's record is now named.

On "because thou hast left thy first love," the witnesses note the lost love. Barnes and Ellicott note the fault: "thou hast left (abandoned, let go) thy first love (the warm, fervent love of their early Christian days)" — the Ephesians had lost the ardent love they had at first; their zeal for orthodoxy and labour remained, but the warmth of their first love for Christ (and perhaps for one another) had cooled; correctness without fervent love.

On "first love," the puritan Henry notes the seriousness: the loss of "first love" is no small matter — love is the heart of religion; orthodoxy, labour, and patience cannot compensate for cooled love; the decline of love is the root of decline, and Christ marks it as a grave fault. The verse displays the rebuke of Ephesus for leaving its first love.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the turn — "nevertheless I have somewhat against thee," the serious fault marring the record despite the praise (Barnes, Robertson); "thou hast left thy first love," the lost warmth of their early love for Christ, correctness without fervency (Barnes, Ellicott); the loss of first love a grave fault, love the heart of religion (Henry). The verse displays the rebuke. The verse marks the complaint: somewhat against thee. The verse displays the fault: left first love. The verse displays the loss: cooled fervency. The verse displays the seriousness: love the heart. The rebuke displays the orthodox church reproved for cooled love. The verse displays the complaint: made. The verse displays the love: left. The verse displays the warmth: lost. The rebuke of Ephesus for leaving its first love displays the one fault amid the praise: despite their works and discernment, Christ has this against them — they had abandoned their first love, the warm fervency of their early devotion. The verse displays the cooled love, calling for repentance lest the lampstand be removed (v.5).
5:
"Remember therefore from whence thou art fallen, and repent, and do the first works; or else I will come unto thee quickly, and will remove thy candlestick out of his place, except thou repent." The witnesses note the call to repent and do the first works, lest the lampstand be removed. On "remember therefore from whence thou art fallen, and repent," the grammatical voices note the threefold cure. Barnes and Robertson note the remedy: "remember (call to mind) from whence (from what height of love) thou art fallen, and repent (change, turn back)" — the cure begins with remembering the former state of love, then repenting of the decline; recollection and repentance are the first steps of recovery.

On "and do the first works," the witnesses note the return to action. Barnes and Ellicott note that they must "do the first works (the deeds that flowed from their first love)" — beyond feeling, they must return to the works of love they did at first; repentance issues in renewed action; the recovery of first love shows in first works.

On "I will come unto thee quickly, and will remove thy candlestick... except thou repent," the puritan Henry notes the threat: if they do not repent, Christ "will come unto thee quickly, and will remove thy candlestick (lampstand) out of his place" — the church that does not recover its love faces the removal of its lampstand; the loss of its standing as a church, the withdrawal of Christ's presence and the gospel light; a solemn warning conditioned on repentance. The verse displays the call to repent and do the first works.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the cure — "remember from whence thou art fallen, and repent," recollection and repentance the first steps of recovery (Barnes, Robertson); "do the first works," returning to the deeds of first love, repentance in action (Barnes, Ellicott); "I will... remove thy candlestick... except thou repent," the threat of the lampstand's removal if they do not repent, the loss of church standing (Henry). The verse displays the call to repent. The verse marks the remembering: whence fallen. The verse displays the repentance: turn back. The verse displays the works: the first. The verse displays the threat: lampstand removed. The call displays repentance and renewed works urged on pain of removal. The verse displays the remembering: urged. The verse displays the repenting: commanded. The verse displays the candlestick: threatened. The call to repent and do the first works displays the remedy and the warning: remember the height from which you fell, repent, and do the first works — or Christ will come and remove the lampstand from its place, unless they repent. The verse displays the threatened lampstand, with a word of praise for hating the Nicolaitans (v.6).
6:
"But this thou hast, that thou hatest the deeds of the Nicolaitans, which I also hate." The brief verse notes Ephesus's commendable hatred of the Nicolaitans' deeds. The grammatical voices note the renewed praise. Barnes and Robertson note the word of commendation closing the reproof: "but this thou hast, that thou hatest the deeds of the Nicolaitans (a sect or party teaching error and licentiousness), which I also hate" — amid the rebuke, Christ commends the church's hatred of the Nicolaitans' deeds; their rejection of this corrupting error is praised.

On "the Nicolaitans," the witnesses note the sect. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "Nicolaitans" were a party (traditionally traced to Nicolas, or named figuratively) whose deeds involved false teaching and immoral practice (cf. vv.14-15, linking them with the error of Balaam); their licentious doctrine corrupted the church; the Ephesians rightly hated it.

On "which I also hate," the puritan Henry notes the shared abhorrence: Christ says "which I also hate" — the church's hatred of the Nicolaitans' deeds matches Christ's own; they hate what Christ hates; this agreement with Christ's mind is commendable; hatred of evil deeds (not of persons) is right and Christlike. The verse displays Ephesus's commendable hatred of the Nicolaitans' deeds.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the praise — "this thou hast, that thou hatest the deeds of the Nicolaitans... which I also hate," Christ commending their hatred of the corrupting error (Barnes, Robertson); the Nicolaitans a party of false, licentious teaching (linked to Balaam, vv.14-15, Barnes, Ellicott); "which I also hate," the church hating what Christ hates, Christlike abhorrence of evil deeds (Henry). The verse displays the commended hatred. The verse marks the praise: this thou hast. The verse displays the hatred: of the Nicolaitans' deeds. The verse displays the sect: false and licentious. The verse displays the agreement: Christ also hates. The praise displays the church hating the evil Christ hates. The verse displays the hatred: commended. The verse displays the deeds: hated. The verse displays the Christ: agreeing. Ephesus's commendable hatred of the Nicolaitans' deeds displays the closing word of commendation: yet this they have, that they hate the deeds of the Nicolaitans, which Christ also hates — their abhorrence of the corrupting error matching his own. The verse displays the hated error, with the promise of the tree of life (v.7).
7:
"He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches; To him that overcometh will I give to eat of the tree of life, which is in the midst of the paradise of God." The witnesses note the call to hear and the promise of the tree of life to the overcomer. On "he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches," the grammatical voices note the universal summons. Barnes and Robertson note the refrain (in all seven letters): "he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches" — the call to attentive hearing, echoing Christ's frequent saying (Matthew 11:15); what is said to one church is for all; the Spirit speaks through the message to every church.

On "the Spirit saith unto the churches," the witnesses note the Spirit's voice. Barnes and Ellicott note that the message, though Christ's, is "what the Spirit saith" — the Holy Spirit speaks in and through the words to the churches; the plural "churches" shows the message is for all, not Ephesus only.

On "to him that overcometh will I give to eat of the tree of life, which is in the midst of the paradise of God," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the promise to the conqueror: "to him that overcometh (the victor, who conquers in the spiritual conflict) will I give to eat of the tree of life, which is in the midst of the paradise of God" — the overcomer is promised the tree of life in God's paradise (recalling Eden, and pointing to 22:2); eternal life and restored access to God's presence; the first of the seven promises to the overcomers. The verse displays the call to hear and the promise of the tree of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches," the refrain calling all to hear, what is said to one for all (Matthew 11:15, Barnes, Robertson); "the Spirit saith," the Holy Spirit speaking through the message to all the churches (Barnes, Ellicott); "to him that overcometh will I give to eat of the tree of life... in the paradise of God," the overcomer promised eternal life and restored access to God (Eden, 22:2, Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promise to the overcomer. The verse marks the summons: hear the Spirit. The verse displays the scope: the churches. The verse displays the promise: the tree of life. The verse displays the place: the paradise of God. The promise displays the conqueror given eternal life in God's paradise. The verse displays the hearing: called. The verse displays the overcomer: rewarded. The verse displays the tree: of life. The call to hear and the promise of the tree of life to the overcomer display the close of the letter to Ephesus: let everyone hear what the Spirit says to the churches — to the one who overcomes, Christ will give to eat of the tree of life in the paradise of God. The verse displays the overcomer's reward, opening the letter to Smyrna (v.8).
8:
"And unto the angel of the church in Smyrna write; These things saith the first and the last, which was dead, and is alive;" The brief verse notes the message to Smyrna from the first and last who died and lives. The grammatical voices note the address and title. Barnes and Robertson note the second letter: "unto the angel of the church in Smyrna write; These things saith the first and the last, which was dead, and is alive" — to the suffering church at Smyrna, Christ presents himself by the title from the vision (1:17-18): the first and the last, who died and is alive again.

On "the first and the last," the witnesses note the divine title. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the first and the last" is the divine title (Isaiah 44:6) applied to Christ, affirming his eternity; the eternal Lord addresses the church facing death.

On "which was dead, and is alive," the puritan Henry notes the suited title: Christ describes himself as the one "which was dead, and is alive (lived again)" — a title especially fitted to comfort a church facing persecution and martyrdom; the Christ who died and rose assures the suffering Smyrnaeans that death is not the end; his resurrection is the pledge of theirs. The verse displays the message to Smyrna from the first and last who died and lives.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the address — "unto the angel of the church in Smyrna write; These things saith the first and the last," the second letter, Christ presenting himself by the title from the vision (Barnes, Robertson); "the first and the last," the divine title affirming Christ's eternity (Isaiah 44:6, Barnes, Ellicott); "which was dead, and is alive," the title suited to comfort the persecuted church, his resurrection the pledge of theirs (Henry). The verse displays the title to Smyrna. The verse marks the address: the angel of Smyrna. The verse displays the title: first and last. The verse displays the death: was dead. The verse displays the life: is alive. The message displays the risen Christ comforting the suffering church. The verse displays the Smyrna: addressed. The verse displays the first and last: eternal. The verse displays the alive: again. The message to Smyrna from the first and last who died and lives displays the opening of the second letter: to the persecuted church at Smyrna, Christ presents himself as the first and the last, who was dead and is alive — the risen Lord who comforts those facing death. The verse displays the risen Christ, who knows Smyrna's tribulation (v.9).
9:
"I know thy works, and tribulation, and poverty, (but thou art rich) and I know the blasphemy of them which say they are Jews, and are not, but are the synagogue of Satan." The witnesses note Christ's knowledge of Smyrna's tribulation, poverty, and rich faith. On "I know thy works, and tribulation, and poverty, (but thou art rich)," the grammatical voices note the paradox. Barnes and Robertson note the searching knowledge and the comfort: "I know thy works, and tribulation (affliction, persecution), and poverty, (but thou art rich)" — Christ knows their suffering and material poverty, yet declares them rich; though poor in this world, they are rich in faith and grace and the treasures of heaven (cf. James 2:5); the paradox of the persecuted poor who are truly rich.

On "(but thou art rich)," the witnesses note the spiritual wealth. Barnes and Ellicott note that the parenthesis corrects the appearance: outwardly poor and afflicted, the Smyrnaeans are inwardly and eternally rich; Christ's estimate, not the world's, defines their true wealth.

On "the blasphemy of them which say they are Jews, and are not, but are the synagogue of Satan," the grammatical-historical note the false Jews: Christ knows "the blasphemy (reviling, slander) of them which say they are Jews, and are not, but are the synagogue of Satan" — those who claimed to be Jews (God's people) but opposed and slandered the church were not true Jews but a "synagogue of Satan"; their persecution of the church marked them as the devil's, not God's. The verse displays Christ's knowledge of Smyrna's tribulation, poverty, and rich faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the paradox — "I know thy works, and tribulation, and poverty, (but thou art rich)," Christ knowing their suffering and poverty yet declaring them rich in faith (James 2:5, Barnes, Robertson); the parenthesis correcting the appearance, the poor truly rich (Barnes, Ellicott); "the blasphemy of them which say they are Jews... the synagogue of Satan," the false Jews who slandered the church marked as the devil's (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rich poor church. The verse marks the knowledge: tribulation and poverty. The verse displays the wealth: but thou art rich. The verse displays the slander: false Jews. The verse displays the verdict: synagogue of Satan. The knowledge displays the afflicted, poor, yet rich church. The verse displays the poverty: known. The verse displays the riches: spiritual. The verse displays the synagogue: of Satan. Christ's knowledge of Smyrna's tribulation, poverty, and rich faith displays the comfort to the suffering church: he knows their affliction and poverty — yet they are rich; and he knows the slander of the false Jews, the synagogue of Satan. The verse displays the rich poor, exhorted to be faithful unto death (v.10).
10:
"Fear none of those things which thou shalt suffer: behold, the devil shall cast some of you into prison, that ye may be tried; and ye shall have tribulation ten days: be thou faithful unto death, and I will give thee a crown of life." The witnesses note the call to be faithful unto death for the crown of life. On "fear none of those things which thou shalt suffer," the grammatical voices note the reassurance. Barnes and Robertson note the encouragement to the suffering church: "fear none of those things which thou shalt suffer" — Christ bids them not to fear the coming sufferings; though persecution lies ahead, they need not dread it; courage in the face of trial.

On "the devil shall cast some of you into prison, that ye may be tried; and ye shall have tribulation ten days," the witnesses note the foretold trial. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the devil shall cast some of you into prison, that ye may be tried (tested), and ye shall have tribulation ten days" — Satan, working through the persecutors, will imprison some, to test their faith; the "ten days" denotes a limited, defined period of tribulation (whether literal or a symbol of a short, bounded time); the trial is permitted but limited.

On "be thou faithful unto death, and I will give thee a crown of life," the puritan Henry notes the charge and promise: "be thou faithful unto death (even to the point of dying), and I will give thee a crown of life" — the call to faithfulness even unto martyrdom, with the promise of the crown of life; faithfulness through death is rewarded with the victor's crown, eternal life; the martyr's reward. The verse displays the call to be faithful unto death for the crown of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reassurance — "fear none of those things which thou shalt suffer," Christ bidding them not to dread the coming sufferings (Barnes, Robertson); "the devil shall cast some... into prison... tribulation ten days," Satan imprisoning some to test their faith, the trial limited to a defined period (Barnes, Ellicott); "be thou faithful unto death, and I will give thee a crown of life," faithfulness even to martyrdom rewarded with the crown of life (Henry). The verse displays the faithful unto death. The verse marks the fear: none. The verse displays the trial: prison, ten days. The verse displays the call: faithful unto death. The verse displays the reward: crown of life. The exhortation displays faithfulness through death crowned with life. The verse displays the suffering: not feared. The verse displays the faithfulness: unto death. The verse displays the crown: of life. The call to be faithful unto death for the crown of life displays the charge to the suffering church: fear not the coming sufferings, though Satan will imprison some and tribulation will last its appointed time — be faithful unto death, and Christ will give the crown of life. The verse displays the crown of life, with the promise of escaping the second death (v.11).
11:
"He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches; He that overcometh shall not be hurt of the second death." The brief verse notes the promise that the overcomer escapes the second death. The grammatical voices note the refrain and promise. Barnes and Robertson note the repeated summons and the reward to the conqueror: "he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches; he that overcometh shall not be hurt of (harmed by) the second death" — the call to hear (as in all seven letters), and the promise that the overcomer will not be harmed by the second death.

On "the second death," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the second death" is the final, eternal death — the lake of fire, eternal perdition (defined in 20:14; 21:8) — as distinct from the first, bodily death; the overcomer, though he may suffer the first death (martyrdom), will never suffer the second.

On "shall not be hurt," the puritan Henry notes the comfort: the overcomer "shall not be hurt (in no way harmed) of the second death" — the martyr who is faithful unto death (v.10) need not fear; though the first death take him, the second death has no power over him; eternal life is secure; the comfort suited to a church facing martyrdom. The verse displays the promise that the overcomer escapes the second death.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the refrain and promise — "he that hath an ear, let him hear... he that overcometh shall not be hurt of the second death," the call to hear and the promise to the conqueror (Barnes, Robertson); "the second death" the final eternal death, the lake of fire (20:14, Barnes, Ellicott); "shall not be hurt," the faithful martyr safe from the second death though the first take him (Henry). The verse displays the overcomer's safety. The verse marks the summons: hear the Spirit. The verse displays the overcomer: the conqueror. The verse displays the promise: no second death. The verse displays the comfort: eternal life secure. The promise displays the martyr untouched by the second death. The verse displays the hearing: called. The verse displays the second death: escaped. The verse displays the overcomer: safe. The promise that the overcomer escapes the second death displays the close of the letter to Smyrna: let everyone hear what the Spirit says — the one who overcomes shall not be harmed by the second death, the eternal death having no power over the faithful. The verse displays the overcomer's safety, opening the letter to Pergamos (v.12).
12:
"And to the angel of the church in Pergamos write; These things saith he which hath the sharp sword with two edges;" The brief verse notes the message to Pergamos from him with the sharp two-edged sword. The grammatical voices note the address and title. Barnes and Robertson note the third letter: "to the angel of the church in Pergamos write; These things saith he which hath the sharp sword with two edges" — to the church at Pergamos, Christ presents himself by the title from the vision (1:16): the one with the sharp two-edged sword.

On "the sharp sword with two edges," the witnesses note the word of judgment. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the sharp sword with two edges" is the word of Christ (1:16; Hebrews 4:12), which proceeds from his mouth; the title is fitted to a church troubled by false teaching (vv.14-15) — Christ comes with the sword of his word to judge and to cut off error.

On the suited title, the puritan Henry notes the relevance: the sword title warns the church that Christ will war against the false teachers among them (v.16) with the sword of his mouth; the title prepares for both the commendation and the rebuke; the word that judges error is held ready. The verse displays the message to Pergamos from him with the sharp two-edged sword.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the address — "to the angel of the church in Pergamos write; These things saith he which hath the sharp sword with two edges," the third letter, Christ presenting himself by the sword title from the vision (1:16, Barnes, Robertson); "the sharp sword with two edges" the word of Christ that judges (Hebrews 4:12), fitted to a church troubled by false teaching (Barnes, Ellicott); the title warning that Christ will war against the false teachers with the sword of his word (Henry). The verse displays the sword title. The verse marks the address: the angel of Pergamos. The verse displays the title: the two-edged sword. The verse displays the meaning: the word of judgment. The verse displays the relevance: against false teaching. The message displays Christ with the sword of his word to Pergamos. The verse displays the Pergamos: addressed. The verse displays the sword: sharp. The verse displays the word: judging. The message to Pergamos from him with the sharp two-edged sword displays the opening of the third letter: to the church at Pergamos, Christ presents himself as the one who has the sharp two-edged sword — the word that judges error, held ready against the false teachers among them. The verse displays the sword of the word, which knows where Pergamos dwells (v.13).
13:
"I know thy works, and where thou dwellest, even where Satan's seat is: and thou holdest fast my name, and hast not denied my faith, even in those days wherein Antipas was my faithful martyr, who was slain among you, where Satan dwelleth." The witnesses note Christ's commendation of Pergamos for holding fast where Satan dwells. On "I know thy works, and where thou dwellest, even where Satan's seat is," the grammatical voices note the hostile setting. Barnes and Robertson note the church's difficult location: "I know thy works, and where thou dwellest, even where Satan's seat (throne) is" — Christ knows their works and the peculiarly hostile place they live; Pergamos was a center of pagan idolatry and emperor worship, called "Satan's seat" (throne) for its concentrated evil and persecution.

On "thou holdest fast my name, and hast not denied my faith," the witnesses note their steadfastness. Barnes and Ellicott note the commendation: "thou holdest fast my name (hast not renounced Christ), and hast not denied my faith (the faith of Christ)" — even in this stronghold of Satan, the church held fast to Christ's name and did not deny the faith; faithfulness under intense pressure.

On "even in those days wherein Antipas was my faithful martyr, who was slain among you," the puritan Henry notes the test: their faithfulness held "even in those days wherein Antipas was my faithful martyr (witness), who was slain among you, where Satan dwelleth" — even when Antipas, a faithful witness, was put to death among them, the church did not abandon Christ; the martyrdom of Antipas proves the reality of their danger and the genuineness of their steadfastness. The verse displays Christ's commendation of Pergamos for holding fast where Satan dwells.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the setting — "I know... where thou dwellest, even where Satan's seat is," the church in a stronghold of idolatry and persecution, "Satan's seat" (Barnes, Robertson); "thou holdest fast my name, and hast not denied my faith," steadfast faithfulness under pressure (Barnes, Ellicott); "even in those days wherein Antipas was my faithful martyr... slain among you," faithfulness held even through the martyrdom of Antipas (Henry). The verse displays the steadfast church. The verse marks the dwelling: Satan's seat. The verse displays the steadfastness: held fast Christ's name. The verse displays the faith: not denied. The verse displays the martyr: Antipas slain. The commendation displays Pergamos faithful in the stronghold of Satan. The verse displays the seat: of Satan. The verse displays the name: held fast. The verse displays the martyr: faithful. Christ's commendation of Pergamos for holding fast where Satan dwells displays the praise of the church in a hostile place: though they dwell where Satan's throne is, they held fast Christ's name and did not deny the faith, even when Antipas the faithful martyr was slain among them. The verse displays the steadfast church, against which a few things are held (v.14).
14:
"But I have a few things against thee, because thou hast there them that hold the doctrine of Balaam, who taught Balac to cast a stumblingblock before the children of Israel, to eat things sacrificed unto idols, and to commit fornication." The witnesses note the rebuke of Pergamos for tolerating the doctrine of Balaam. On "I have a few things against thee, because thou hast there them that hold the doctrine of Balaam," the grammatical voices note the fault. Barnes and Robertson note the complaint: "I have a few things against thee, because thou hast there them that hold the doctrine (teaching) of Balaam" — despite their steadfastness, the church tolerated within it those who held the corrupting teaching associated with Balaam; the toleration of false teachers is the fault.

On "who taught Balac to cast a stumblingblock before the children of Israel," the witnesses note the historical type. Barnes and Ellicott note the reference (Numbers 25; 31:16): Balaam "taught Balak to cast a stumblingblock before the children of Israel" — Balaam counseled Balak to seduce Israel into sin (idolatry and immorality) when he could not curse them; the type of the false teacher who corrupts God's people through enticement.

On "to eat things sacrificed unto idols, and to commit fornication," the grammatical-historical note the specific errors: the Balaam-like teaching led God's people "to eat things sacrificed unto idols, and to commit fornication" — idolatrous feasting and immorality; the same licentious error (linked to the Nicolaitans, v.15) that the Balaam party promoted; toleration of this corruption is what Christ rebukes. The verse displays the rebuke of Pergamos for tolerating the doctrine of Balaam.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fault — "I have a few things against thee, because thou hast there them that hold the doctrine of Balaam," the toleration of false teachers within the church (Barnes, Robertson); "Balaam... taught Balak to cast a stumblingblock before Israel" (Numbers 25; 31:16), the type of the false teacher who corrupts by enticement (Barnes, Ellicott); "to eat things sacrificed unto idols, and to commit fornication," idolatrous feasting and immorality, the licentious error tolerated (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the tolerated error. The verse marks the complaint: a few things. The verse displays the fault: the doctrine of Balaam. The verse displays the type: Balaam and Balak. The verse displays the errors: idol-feasts and fornication. The rebuke displays Pergamos tolerating the corrupting Balaam teaching. The verse displays the doctrine: of Balaam. The verse displays the stumblingblock: cast. The verse displays the fornication: promoted. The rebuke of Pergamos for tolerating the doctrine of Balaam displays the fault amid the praise: though steadfast, the church harbored those holding the teaching of Balaam — who taught Balak to ensnare Israel into eating idol-sacrifices and committing fornication. The verse displays the Balaam error, joined to the Nicolaitans (v.15).
15:
"So hast thou also them that hold the doctrine of the Nicolaitans, which thing I hate." The brief verse notes Pergamos's toleration of the Nicolaitans' doctrine, which Christ hates. The grammatical voices note the parallel error. Barnes and Robertson note the linking of the two: "so hast thou also them that hold the doctrine of the Nicolaitans" — alongside the Balaam party, the church at Pergamos also harbored those holding the doctrine of the Nicolaitans; the two are connected (both teaching idolatry and immorality); Pergamos tolerated what Ephesus hated (2:6).

On "the doctrine of the Nicolaitans," the witnesses note the same corruption. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "doctrine of the Nicolaitans" is the licentious teaching (like Balaam's) that countenanced idol-feasts and immorality; the parallel with the doctrine of Balaam (v.14) suggests they are the same or closely related corrupting error.

On "which thing I hate," the puritan Henry notes Christ's abhorrence: "which thing I hate" — Christ declares his hatred of the Nicolaitan doctrine (as in 2:6); the church's toleration of what Christ hates is the gravity of their fault; to harbor what Christ hates is to share its guilt unless repented of. The verse displays Pergamos's toleration of the Nicolaitans' doctrine, which Christ hates.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the parallel — "so hast thou also them that hold the doctrine of the Nicolaitans," the church also harboring the Nicolaitans, linked to the Balaam party (Barnes, Robertson); the Nicolaitan doctrine the same licentious corruption as Balaam's (Barnes, Ellicott); "which thing I hate," Christ's abhorrence of the doctrine, the church's toleration of what he hates the gravity of the fault (Henry). The verse displays the tolerated Nicolaitans. The verse marks the toleration: the Nicolaitans. The verse displays the link: with Balaam. The verse displays the corruption: licentious doctrine. The verse displays the abhorrence: which I hate. The rebuke displays Pergamos tolerating what Christ hates. The verse displays the Nicolaitans: held. The verse displays the doctrine: corrupting. The verse displays the hatred: Christ's. Pergamos's toleration of the Nicolaitans' doctrine, which Christ hates, displays the second part of the rebuke: so too the church harbored those holding the doctrine of the Nicolaitans — the licentious corruption that Christ hates; toleration of it is the fault to be repented. The verse displays the hated doctrine, calling for repentance lest Christ war with the sword (v.16).
16:
"Repent; or else I will come unto thee quickly, and will fight against them with the sword of my mouth." The brief verse notes the call to repent, lest Christ war with the sword of his mouth. The grammatical voices note the command and threat. Barnes and Robertson note the urgent call: "repent; or else I will come unto thee quickly, and will fight against them with the sword of my mouth" — the church is commanded to repent (of its toleration of the false teachers); if not, Christ will come quickly and war against the false teachers ("them") with the sword of his mouth.

On "repent," the witnesses note the duty. Barnes and Ellicott note that the whole church is called to "repent" — to put away the toleration of the corrupting error; repentance here means removing or disciplining the false teachers, not merely personal sorrow.

On "I will... fight against them with the sword of my mouth," the puritan Henry notes the judgment: Christ "will fight against them (the false teachers) with the sword of my mouth (his word, 1:16; 2:12)" — if the church will not deal with the error, Christ himself will come and judge the false teachers by the word of his mouth; the sword title (v.12) is now turned against them; the word that was held ready will smite the impenitent. The verse displays the call to repent, lest Christ war with the sword of his mouth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "repent; or else I will come unto thee quickly," the church called to repent of its toleration of error (Barnes, Robertson); "repent" meaning removing or disciplining the false teachers, not mere sorrow (Barnes, Ellicott); "fight against them with the sword of my mouth," Christ judging the false teachers by his word if the church will not (1:16, Henry). The verse displays the call to repent. The verse marks the command: repent. The verse displays the threat: I will come. The verse displays the war: against the false teachers. The verse displays the weapon: the sword of his mouth. The call displays repentance urged on pain of Christ's judgment. The verse displays the repentance: commanded. The verse displays the coming: quickly. The verse displays the sword: of his mouth. The call to repent, lest Christ war with the sword of his mouth, displays the warning to Pergamos: repent of tolerating the false teaching — or Christ will come quickly and fight against the false teachers with the sword of his mouth. The verse displays the warring word, with the promise of hidden manna and a white stone (v.17).
17:
"He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches; To him that overcometh will I give to eat of the hidden manna, and will give him a white stone, and in the stone a new name written, which no man knoweth saving he that receiveth it." The witnesses note the promise of hidden manna and a white stone with a new name. On "he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches," the grammatical voices note the refrain. Barnes and Robertson note the repeated summons (as in all the letters) calling all to hear what the Spirit says.

On "to him that overcometh will I give to eat of the hidden manna," the witnesses note the first promise. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the hidden manna" is the secret, spiritual nourishment of the overcomer — recalling the manna hidden in the ark; the promise of Christ himself as the true bread, the hidden spiritual food and life given to the conqueror (cf. John 6).

On "a white stone, and in the stone a new name written, which no man knoweth saving he that receiveth it," the grammatical-historical note the white stone. Barnes and Robertson note the much-discussed figure: "a white stone (psephos), and in the stone a new name written, which no man knoweth saving he that receiveth it" — interpreters offer several views (a token of acquittal, as a white stone in a vote; a token of admission or favour; the victor's token); the white stone with the secret new name signifies the overcomer's acceptance, honour, and a personal, intimate relation to Christ known only to himself; the new name marks the new, glorified standing. The verse displays the promise of hidden manna and a white stone with a new name.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the refrain — "he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches," the summons calling all to hear (Barnes, Robertson); "the hidden manna," the secret spiritual nourishment, Christ the true bread to the overcomer (John 6, Barnes, Ellicott); "a white stone... a new name... which no man knoweth," the white stone of acceptance and honour, the secret new name marking intimate relation to Christ and the new standing (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the overcomer's reward. The verse marks the summons: hear the Spirit. The verse displays the manna: hidden. The verse displays the stone: white. The verse displays the name: new, secret. The promise displays the conqueror given hidden food and a secret name. The verse displays the manna: given. The verse displays the stone: bestowed. The verse displays the name: new. The promise of hidden manna and a white stone with a new name displays the close of the letter to Pergamos: to the overcomer Christ gives the hidden manna and a white stone bearing a new name, known only to its receiver — the secret nourishment, acceptance, and intimate honour of the conqueror. The verse displays the overcomer's reward, opening the letter to Thyatira (v.18).
18:
"And unto the angel of the church in Thyatira write; These things saith the Son of God, who hath his eyes like unto a flame of fire, and his feet are like fine brass;" The brief verse notes the message to Thyatira from the Son of God with eyes of fire and feet of brass. The grammatical voices note the address and title. Barnes and Robertson note the fourth letter: "unto the angel of the church in Thyatira write; These things saith the Son of God, who hath his eyes like unto a flame of fire, and his feet are like fine brass" — to the church at Thyatira, Christ presents himself by the title "the Son of God" (the only letter where this exact title appears) and by features from the vision (1:14-15): eyes of flaming fire and feet of burnished brass.

On "the Son of God... eyes like unto a flame of fire," the witnesses note the searching gaze. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the Son of God" affirms his deity and authority, and the "eyes like a flame of fire" signify his penetrating, all-searching knowledge — fitting for a church to be searched and judged for its toleration of error (vv.20-23); nothing is hidden from his flaming eyes.

On "his feet are like fine brass," the grammatical-historical note the feet of judgment: "his feet are like fine brass (glowing bronze)" — the feet of strength and judgment (1:15), fitted to one who comes to tread down evil; the title prepares for the searching judgment of the church and the false prophetess. The verse displays the message to Thyatira from the Son of God with eyes of fire and feet of brass.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the address — "unto the angel of the church in Thyatira write; These things saith the Son of God, who hath his eyes like unto a flame of fire, and his feet are like fine brass," the fourth letter, Christ presenting himself as the Son of God with features from the vision (Barnes, Robertson); "the Son of God... eyes like a flame of fire," his deity and all-searching knowledge, fitted to search and judge (Barnes, Ellicott); "his feet like fine brass," the feet of strength and judgment, treading down evil (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the searching Christ. The verse marks the address: the angel of Thyatira. The verse displays the title: the Son of God. The verse displays the eyes: flame of fire. The verse displays the feet: fine brass. The message displays the Son of God searching and judging the church. The verse displays the Thyatira: addressed. The verse displays the eyes: searching. The verse displays the feet: judging. The message to Thyatira from the Son of God with eyes of fire and feet of brass displays the opening of the fourth letter: to the church at Thyatira, Christ presents himself as the Son of God, whose eyes are a flame of fire and whose feet are like burnished brass — the searching, judging Lord. The verse displays the searching Son of God, who knows Thyatira's works and love (v.19).
19:
"I know thy works, and charity, and service, and faith, and thy patience, and thy works; and the last to be more than the first." The brief verse notes Christ's commendation of Thyatira's growing works of love and faith. The grammatical voices note the fivefold praise. Barnes and Robertson note the commendation: "I know thy works, and charity (love), and service (ministry), and faith, and thy patience (endurance), and thy works" — Christ knows the church's deeds, marked by love, ministry, faith, and patient endurance; a fuller, warmer commendation than Ephesus, who had left their first love — Thyatira abounds in love and service.

On "charity, and service, and faith, and thy patience," the witnesses note the graces. Barnes and Ellicott note the cluster of virtues: love, service (active ministry to others), faith, and patience; the church was rich in the practical graces of Christian life, especially love and works flowing from it.

On "the last to be more than the first," the puritan Henry notes the growth: "the last to be more than the first" — unlike Ephesus (whose love had declined), Thyatira's later works exceeded their earlier; they were growing, not declining, in love and service; the commendation of increasing fruitfulness. The verse displays Christ's commendation of Thyatira's growing works of love and faith.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the praise — "I know thy works, and charity (love), and service, and faith, and thy patience, and thy works," Christ commending their love, ministry, faith, and endurance, a warmer praise than Ephesus (Barnes, Robertson); the cluster of practical graces, the church rich in love and works (Barnes, Ellicott); "the last to be more than the first," their later works exceeding the earlier, growing not declining (Henry). The verse displays the growing church. The verse marks the works: known. The verse displays the graces: love, service, faith, patience. The verse displays the growth: last more than first. The verse displays the contrast: with Ephesus. The commendation displays Thyatira increasing in love and service. The verse displays the charity: praised. The verse displays the service: abounding. The verse displays the growth: increasing. Christ's commendation of Thyatira's growing works of love and faith displays the warm praise of the church: he knows their works, love, service, faith, and patience — and their last works are more than the first; they grow in love and service, unlike the declining Ephesus. The verse displays the growing love, against which one grave fault is held (v.20).
20:
"Notwithstanding I have a few things against thee, because thou sufferest that woman Jezebel, which calleth herself a prophetess, to teach and to seduce my servants to commit fornication, and to eat things sacrificed unto idols." The witnesses note the rebuke of Thyatira for tolerating the false prophetess Jezebel. On "I have a few things against thee, because thou sufferest that woman Jezebel," the grammatical voices note the fault. Barnes and Robertson note the complaint: "I have a few things against thee, because thou sufferest (tolerate, permit) that woman Jezebel, which calleth herself a prophetess" — despite their growing love, the church tolerated a woman (called "Jezebel," after Ahab's idolatrous queen) who claimed to be a prophetess; the toleration of this false teacher is the fault.

On "Jezebel... a prophetess," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Ellicott note that "Jezebel" (whether a real woman so named, or named figuratively after the Old Testament Jezebel, 1 Kings 16; 21) was a false prophetess in the church, who, like her namesake, promoted idolatry and immorality; she claimed divine inspiration to teach error.

On "to teach and to seduce my servants to commit fornication, and to eat things sacrificed unto idols," the puritan Henry notes the corruption: she was permitted "to teach and to seduce (lead astray) my servants to commit fornication, and to eat things sacrificed unto idols" — the same licentious error as the Nicolaitans and Balaam (idolatry and immorality); she seduced Christ's servants into sin; the church's fault was tolerating her teaching and influence. The verse displays the rebuke of Thyatira for tolerating the false prophetess Jezebel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fault — "thou sufferest that woman Jezebel, which calleth herself a prophetess," the toleration of a self-styled prophetess in the church (Barnes, Robertson); "Jezebel" named after Ahab's idolatrous queen (1 Kings 16; 21), promoting idolatry and immorality (Barnes, Ellicott); "to teach and to seduce my servants to commit fornication, and to eat things sacrificed unto idols," the same licentious corruption, seducing Christ's servants (Henry). The verse displays the tolerated prophetess. The verse marks the complaint: a few things. The verse displays the fault: suffering Jezebel. The verse displays the figure: the false prophetess. The verse displays the corruption: idolatry and fornication. The rebuke displays Thyatira tolerating the seducing Jezebel. The verse displays the Jezebel: tolerated. The verse displays the seduction: of the servants. The verse displays the error: licentious. The rebuke of Thyatira for tolerating the false prophetess Jezebel displays the grave fault amid the praise: despite their love and growth, the church permitted the woman Jezebel, who claimed prophecy, to teach and seduce Christ's servants into fornication and idol-feasts. The verse displays the tolerated Jezebel, given space to repent but refusing (v.21).
21:
"And I gave her space to repent of her fornication; and she repented not." The brief verse notes that Jezebel was given space to repent but refused. The grammatical voices note God's patience. Barnes and Robertson note the forbearance shown: "I gave her space (time, opportunity) to repent of her fornication; and she repented not (would not repent)" — Christ had given the false prophetess time and opportunity to repent of her sin, but she refused; God's patience preceded his judgment, but she hardened herself against it.

On "space to repent," the witnesses note the divine longsuffering. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "space to repent" shows God's mercy even to the corrupt; he does not judge without first giving opportunity for repentance; the delay of judgment was an offer of grace.

On "she repented not," the puritan Henry notes the hardening: "she repented not" — despite the space given, she persisted impenitently in her sin; her refusal to repent, after being granted opportunity, justifies the judgment that follows (vv.22-23); impenitence after mercy seals the doom. The verse displays that Jezebel was given space to repent but refused.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the forbearance — "I gave her space to repent of her fornication; and she repented not," Christ giving the prophetess time to repent, but she refused (Barnes, Robertson); the "space to repent" showing God's mercy even to the corrupt, the delay an offer of grace (Barnes, Ellicott); "she repented not," her impenitence after mercy justifying the coming judgment (Henry). The verse displays the refused repentance. The verse marks the space: to repent. The verse displays the patience: God's. The verse displays the refusal: repented not. The verse displays the hardening: impenitent. The forbearance displays Jezebel refusing the offered repentance. The verse displays the space: given. The verse displays the repentance: refused. The verse displays the judgment: justified. That Jezebel was given space to repent but refused displays God's patience and her hardening: Christ gave her time to repent of her fornication, but she would not — her impenitence after mercy sealing the judgment to come. The verse displays the impenitent prophetess, threatened with a bed of affliction (v.22).
22:
"Behold, I will cast her into a bed, and them that commit adultery with her into great tribulation, except they repent of their deeds." The brief verse notes the judgment on Jezebel and her followers, except they repent. The grammatical voices note the sentence. Barnes and Robertson note the threatened judgment: "behold, I will cast her into a bed (of sickness or affliction, a grim contrast to the bed of her sin), and them that commit adultery with her (her followers in idolatry and immorality) into great tribulation" — Christ will judge the impenitent prophetess with affliction, and her followers with great tribulation; the judgment fits the sin.

On "cast her into a bed," the witnesses note the irony. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "bed" into which she is cast is a bed of sickness or suffering — a fitting, ironic judgment on the bed of her adultery; her sin becomes the instrument of her punishment.

On "except they repent of their deeds," the puritan Henry notes the open door: even now the judgment is conditional — "except they repent of their deeds" — her followers (though perhaps not she, who had refused, v.21) may yet escape by repentance; the threat is also a call; mercy is still offered to those who turn. The verse displays the judgment on Jezebel and her followers, except they repent.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sentence — "I will cast her into a bed, and them that commit adultery with her into great tribulation," the prophetess judged with affliction and her followers with tribulation (Barnes, Robertson); the "bed" a bed of sickness, an ironic judgment on the bed of her sin (Barnes, Ellicott); "except they repent of their deeds," the judgment conditional, mercy still offered to her followers who repent (Henry). The verse displays the conditional judgment. The verse marks the sentence: cast into a bed. The verse displays the followers: great tribulation. The verse displays the irony: the bed of sin. The verse displays the condition: except they repent. The judgment displays Jezebel and her followers threatened unless they turn. The verse displays the bed: of affliction. The verse displays the tribulation: great. The verse displays the repentance: still offered. The judgment on Jezebel and her followers, except they repent, displays the sentence on the impenitent: Christ will cast Jezebel into a bed of affliction and her followers into great tribulation — unless they repent of their deeds. The verse displays the threatened judgment, sealed by the death of her children (v.23).
23:
"And I will kill her children with death; and all the churches shall know that I am he which searcheth the reins and hearts: and I will give unto every one of you according to your works." The witnesses note the judgment on Jezebel's children and Christ as searcher of hearts. On "I will kill her children with death," the grammatical voices note the severe sentence. Barnes and Robertson note the judgment on her followers: "I will kill her children (her spiritual offspring, those wholly given to her doctrine) with death (with pestilence, or sure destruction)" — those fully committed to Jezebel's corruption ("her children," distinct from the followers who might repent, v.22) will be destroyed; the decisive judgment on the confirmed adherents of the error.

On "all the churches shall know that I am he which searcheth the reins and hearts," the witnesses note Christ's omniscience. Barnes and Ellicott note the effect: "all the churches shall know that I am he which searcheth the reins (kidneys, the inmost feelings) and hearts" — the judgment will make all the churches know that Christ searches the inmost being; the searching eyes of fire (v.18) penetrate the heart; the divine prerogative of searching hearts (Jeremiah 17:10) is Christ's.

On "I will give unto every one of you according to your works," the puritan Henry notes the just recompense: Christ "will give unto every one of you according to your works" — the principle of just judgment; each will be rewarded or punished according to his works; the searcher of hearts renders to each his due. The verse displays the judgment on Jezebel's children and Christ as searcher of hearts.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sentence — "I will kill her children with death," the confirmed adherents of Jezebel's error destroyed (Barnes, Robertson); "all the churches shall know that I am he which searcheth the reins and hearts," the judgment manifesting Christ's omniscience, searching the inmost being (Jeremiah 17:10, Barnes, Ellicott); "I will give unto every one of you according to your works," just recompense to each according to his works (Henry). The verse displays the searching judge. The verse marks the children: killed. The verse displays the searcher: of hearts. The verse displays the omniscience: reins and hearts. The verse displays the recompense: according to works. The judgment displays Christ searching hearts and rendering to each his due. The verse displays the children: judged. The verse displays the searching: of the heart. The verse displays the works: recompensed. The judgment on Jezebel's children and Christ as searcher of hearts displays the decisive sentence and its lesson: Christ will destroy her confirmed children, and all the churches will know that he searches the inmost heart and gives to each according to his works. The verse displays the heart-searching Christ, with a lighter word to the faithful remnant (v.24).
24:
"But unto you I say, and unto the rest in Thyatira, as many as have not this doctrine, and which have not known the depths of Satan, as they speak; I will put upon you none other burden." The witnesses note the lighter word to the faithful remnant who rejected the error. On "unto you... and unto the rest in Thyatira, as many as have not this doctrine," the grammatical voices note the faithful remnant. Barnes and Robertson note the turn to the faithful: "unto you... and unto the rest in Thyatira, as many as have not this doctrine (have not embraced Jezebel's teaching)" — Christ addresses the faithful remnant who rejected the corrupting doctrine; they are distinguished from Jezebel and her followers.

On "which have not known the depths of Satan, as they speak," the witnesses note the rejected error. Barnes and Ellicott note the phrase: "have not known the depths of Satan, as they speak" — the false teachers may have boasted of knowing "the deep things" (claiming hidden, advanced knowledge), which John calls "the depths of Satan"; the faithful had not gone into these so-called deep mysteries, which were really satanic; their ignorance of the error is their commendation.

On "I will put upon you none other burden," the puritan Henry notes the gracious relief: "I will put upon you none other burden" — Christ lays no further burden on the faithful than to hold fast (v.25); he does not impose new demands but bids them keep what they have; the gentleness of Christ toward the faithful, who are not weighed down but encouraged. The verse displays the lighter word to the faithful remnant who rejected the error.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the remnant — "unto the rest in Thyatira, as many as have not this doctrine," the faithful who rejected Jezebel's teaching, distinguished from her followers (Barnes, Robertson); "have not known the depths of Satan, as they speak," the false teachers' boasted "deep things" being really satanic, which the faithful avoided (Barnes, Ellicott); "I will put upon you none other burden," Christ laying no further burden but to hold fast, his gentleness to the faithful (Henry). The verse displays the faithful remnant. The verse marks the remnant: the rest. The verse displays the rejection: not this doctrine. The verse displays the error: the depths of Satan. The verse displays the relief: no other burden. The word displays the faithful spared further burden. The verse displays the remnant: faithful. The verse displays the depths: of Satan. The verse displays the burden: none added. The lighter word to the faithful remnant who rejected the error displays Christ's gentleness to the faithful: to the rest in Thyatira who had not embraced Jezebel's doctrine nor the so-called depths of Satan, Christ lays no further burden. The verse displays the unburdened faithful, called only to hold fast till he comes (v.25).
25:
"But that which ye have already hold fast till I come." The brief verse notes the call to hold fast what they have till Christ comes. The grammatical voices note the simple charge. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation to the faithful: "but that which ye have already (the faith and good works you possess) hold fast (keep, retain firmly) till I come" — the faithful are simply to hold fast what they have until Christ comes; no new burden, only steadfast perseverance in the good they already possess.

On "hold fast," the witnesses note the perseverance. Barnes and Ellicott note that "hold fast" is the call to firm perseverance — to retain their faith, love, and good works without letting go; the faithful need not attain something new but must not lose what they have.

On "till I come," the puritan Henry notes the goal: "till I come" — the holding fast is to continue until Christ's coming; whether his coming in judgment on the church or his final advent, the perseverance is to last to the end; the coming of Christ is the horizon of their steadfastness. The verse displays the call to hold fast what they have till Christ comes.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "that which ye have already hold fast (retain firmly) till I come," the faithful to hold fast what they possess until Christ comes (Barnes, Robertson); "hold fast" the call to firm perseverance, not losing their faith and works (Barnes, Ellicott); "till I come," the perseverance lasting until Christ's coming (Henry). The verse displays the holding fast. The verse marks the possession: what ye have. The verse displays the charge: hold fast. The verse displays the duration: till I come. The verse displays the perseverance: steadfast. The call displays the faithful keeping what they have to the end. The verse displays the holding: fast. The verse displays the having: already. The verse displays the coming: of Christ. The call to hold fast what they have till Christ comes displays the gentle charge to the faithful remnant: simply hold fast the faith and good works you already possess until Christ comes — steadfast perseverance to the end. The verse displays the held fast faith, rewarded with power over the nations (vv.26-27).
26:
"And he that overcometh, and keepeth my works unto the end, to him will I give power over the nations:" The brief verse notes the promise of power over the nations to the overcomer. The grammatical voices note the conqueror's reward. Barnes and Robertson note the promise: "he that overcometh, and keepeth my works (does the works Christ commands) unto the end, to him will I give power (authority, exousian) over the nations" — to the one who conquers and perseveres in Christ's works to the end, Christ promises authority over the nations; the overcomer shares Christ's rule.

On "keepeth my works unto the end," the witnesses note the perseverance. Barnes and Ellicott note that the promise is to the one who not only overcomes but "keeps my works unto the end" — perseverance in obedience to the close; the reward belongs to enduring faithfulness, not a momentary victory.

On "power over the nations," the grammatical-historical note the shared dominion: "power over the nations" — the overcomer is given to share in Christ's royal authority over the nations (drawn from Psalm 2:8-9); the saints reign with Christ; the promise of participation in his kingly rule, fulfilled in his kingdom. The verse displays the promise of power over the nations to the overcomer.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reward — "he that overcometh, and keepeth my works unto the end, to him will I give power over the nations," authority over the nations to the persevering conqueror (Barnes, Robertson); "keepeth my works unto the end," the reward to enduring faithfulness, not momentary victory (Barnes, Ellicott); "power over the nations," sharing Christ's royal authority (Psalm 2:8-9), reigning with him (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the overcomer's rule. The verse marks the overcomer: and keeper of works. The verse displays the perseverance: unto the end. The verse displays the reward: power over the nations. The verse displays the dominion: shared with Christ. The promise displays the conqueror given a share in Christ's rule. The verse displays the overcoming: rewarded. The verse displays the works: kept. The verse displays the power: granted. The promise of power over the nations to the overcomer displays the reward of perseverance: the one who overcomes and keeps Christ's works to the end will be given authority over the nations — a share in Christ's royal rule. The verse displays the overcomer's authority, ruling with a rod of iron (v.27).
27:
"And he shall rule them with a rod of iron; as the vessels of a potter shall they be broken to shivers: even as I received of my Father." The brief verse notes the overcomer ruling the nations with Christ's authority. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note the Messianic promise applied to the overcomer: "he shall rule (shepherd, poimanei) them with a rod of iron; as the vessels of a potter shall they be broken to shivers (shattered)" — drawn from Psalm 2:9, the promise that the overcomer, sharing Christ's rule, will govern the nations with firm, irresistible authority; the rod of iron and the shattered pottery signify the unbreakable, decisive sovereignty.

On "rule them with a rod of iron," the witnesses note the firm dominion. Barnes and Ellicott note that "a rod of iron" signifies strong, unyielding rule — authority that cannot be resisted or broken; the nations are ruled with absolute power; the figure of the shepherd's rod turned to iron, ruling and, where needed, crushing opposition.

On "even as I received of my Father," the eastern Cyril notes the source: the overcomer's authority is "even as I received of my Father" — Christ shares with the overcomer the very authority he himself received from the Father; the saints' rule is a participation in Christ's God-given dominion; the derived, shared sovereignty. The verse displays the overcomer ruling the nations with Christ's authority.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the citation — "he shall rule them with a rod of iron; as the vessels of a potter shall they be broken to shivers," the Messianic promise (Psalm 2:9) of firm, irresistible rule given to the overcomer (Barnes, Robertson); "a rod of iron" signifying strong, unyielding dominion that crushes opposition (Barnes, Ellicott); "even as I received of my Father," the overcomer sharing the authority Christ received from the Father (Cyril). The verse displays the shared rule. The verse marks the ruling: with a rod of iron. The verse displays the figure: broken pottery. The verse displays the authority: firm, irresistible. The verse displays the source: received of the Father. The promise displays the overcomer sharing Christ's God-given rule. The verse displays the rod: of iron. The verse displays the nations: ruled. The verse displays the authority: shared. The overcomer ruling the nations with Christ's authority displays the fullness of the promise: the conqueror shall rule the nations with a rod of iron, shattering opposition like a potter's vessels — even as Christ received this authority from his Father. The verse displays the shared dominion, crowned with the morning star (v.28).
28:
"And I will give him the morning star." The brief verse notes the promise of the morning star to the overcomer. The grammatical voices note the gift. Barnes and Robertson note the crowning promise: "I will give him the morning star" — to the overcomer Christ promises "the morning star"; the meaning is much discussed, but the dominant view takes it as Christ himself, who calls himself "the bright and morning star" (22:16); to be given the morning star is to possess Christ in his glory.

On "the morning star," the witnesses note the interpretations. Barnes and Ellicott note the views: the morning star may signify Christ himself (the giving of Christ in his glorified fullness, 22:16); or the splendour, glory, and immortality given to the overcomer (the bright star of dawn as the emblem of the coming glory and the resurrection); the brightest reward, surpassing even rule over the nations.

On the gift, the eastern Cyril notes the glory: the morning star, herald of the day, signifies the dawning of eternal glory upon the overcomer; Christ gives himself, the true light, the bright and morning star, as the conqueror's everlasting portion and joy. The verse displays the promise of the morning star to the overcomer.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gift — "I will give him the morning star," the crowning promise to the overcomer (Barnes, Robertson); "the morning star" read chiefly as Christ himself (the bright and morning star, 22:16), or as the splendour, glory, and immortality given the conqueror (Barnes, Ellicott); the morning star the dawning of eternal glory, Christ given as the conqueror's portion (Cyril). The verse displays the crowning gift. The verse marks the gift: the morning star. The verse displays the meaning: Christ himself, or glory. The verse displays the splendour: the dawn of glory. The verse displays the reward: the brightest. The promise displays the overcomer given Christ the morning star. The verse displays the star: morning. The verse displays the gift: bestowed. The verse displays the glory: dawning. The promise of the morning star to the overcomer displays the crowning reward of the letter to Thyatira: to the one who overcomes Christ will give the morning star — Christ himself in his glory, the dawn of eternal splendour upon the conqueror. The verse displays the morning star, closing with the call to hear the Spirit (v.29).
29:
"He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches." The brief verse notes the closing call to hear what the Spirit says to the churches. The grammatical voices note the refrain. Barnes and Robertson note the repeated summons closing the letter (and each of the seven): "he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches" — the call to attentive hearing, the same formula concluding the letters to the last four churches (placed after the promise, whereas in the first three it precedes it); the Spirit's word to all the churches demands a hearing.

On "he that hath an ear," the witnesses note the universal address. Barnes and Ellicott note that "he that hath an ear" addresses everyone capable of hearing — all who will heed; the message, though to Thyatira, is for all the churches and all believers.

On "what the Spirit saith unto the churches," the grammatical-historical note the Spirit's voice: the message is "what the Spirit saith unto the churches" — the Holy Spirit speaks through Christ's words to all the churches; the plural marks the universal application; what was said to Thyatira is the Spirit's word to every church in every age. The verse displays the closing call to hear what the Spirit says to the churches.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the refrain — "he that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches," the summons closing the letter and calling all to hear (Barnes, Robertson); "he that hath an ear" addressing all who will heed, the message for all the churches (Barnes, Ellicott); "what the Spirit saith unto the churches," the Holy Spirit's word to every church, universally applicable (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the closing summons. The verse marks the call: hear the Spirit. The verse displays the address: all who hear. The verse displays the speaker: the Spirit. The verse displays the scope: the churches. The summons displays the Spirit's word to all the churches demanding a hearing. The verse displays the ear: to hear. The verse displays the Spirit: speaking. The verse displays the churches: all. The closing call to hear what the Spirit says to the churches displays the refrain that ends the letter to Thyatira: let everyone who has an ear hear what the Spirit says to the churches — the Spirit's word to Thyatira a word for all the churches. The verse displays the Spirit's call, closing the letters to the first four churches.
